title: Registry never validates phases.json — schema module exists but is unused
description: registry.ts imports validatePath but never imports or invokes validatePhases. The contract names phases.json as a first-class shape contract (Files line for schemas/phases.ts, A5 + the contract behavior 'lists every well-formed path it discovers'), and a path is by definition phase-bearing content. Today a directory with a valid path.json but a corrupt or absent phases.json is reported as a healthy PathInfo. The schemas/phases.ts module is therefore dead code at the registry boundary — it only exists for direct test calls. This is an integration gap between two engine modules that were designed to compose.
impact: A future cycle (4) that consumes phases.json will either discover the malformed file at lesson-render time (late failure) or have to re-scan the directory itself, duplicating registry responsibility. Worse, downstream code can rely on the registry's surface as 'these paths are valid' when in reality only path.json was checked. The 'self-contained content' promise of paths/<slug>/ is broken because the engine doesn't enforce its own structural invariants.
recommendation: In scanRegistry, after validatePath succeeds, also read paths/<slug>/phases.json and run validatePhases on it. On failure, push a structured warning (kind 'malformed-phases-json' / 'invalid-phases-json') and skip the path — same flow as path.json. This keeps the schemas module load-bearing and makes the registry the single chokepoint for path well-formedness.

title: RegistryWarning kind is loosely typed as string — discriminator union is the natural shape
description: RegistryWarning declares `kind: string` and an open optional grab-bag of `path?: string` and `dir?: string`. The contract enumerates a closed set of warning kinds: 'no-paths-dir', 'empty-paths-dir', 'missing-path-json', 'malformed-path-json', 'invalid-path-json' (plus the phases-related ones if R2-001 is fixed). A discriminated union (kind: 'no-paths-dir' | 'empty-paths-dir' | ...) with per-kind required fields would let TypeScript catch a typo like 'malformed-paths-json' (note the trailing 's') at compile time and would let callers (skill renderer, future tools) exhaustively switch on kind. Right now any consumer that wants to format a warning has to defensively check both `path` and `dir` because the type system never tells them which one is set.
impact: Encourages stringly-typed branching in downstream renderers (skills/course-engine, future tools) and silently allows the producer side to drift from the documented vocabulary. Hurts the 'engine extensibility without coupling' design goal: a new warning kind cannot be added in one place — the producer and every consumer pay the cost.
recommendation: Replace the open shape with a tagged union, e.g. `type RegistryWarning = { kind: 'no-paths-dir'; message: string } | { kind: 'empty-paths-dir'; message: string } | { kind: 'missing-path-json'; message: string; dir: string } | { kind: 'malformed-path-json' | 'invalid-path-json'; message: string; path: string }`. Export the union from registry.ts so start.ts and skill renderers can re-export and exhaustive-check.

title: OutputStyleResult and RegistryWarning are two parallel warning shapes with no shared contract
description: outputStyle.ts defines its own `OutputStyleWarning { kind, message }` and registry.ts defines `RegistryWarning { kind, message, path?, dir? }`. The skill renderer has to know about both shapes; today start.ts silently drops the outputStyle warning entirely (it only reads styleResult.ok and discards styleResult.warning). That dropped-warning data flow is invisible from the StartResult type. Either both should share a base 'EngineWarning' shape and merge into start.warnings, or the design should make the asymmetry explicit (e.g. a separate top-level outputStyleWarning slot in StartResult).
impact: (1) The user never sees 'settings-file-malformed' surfaced through the skill — diagnostic value is lost. (2) Two parallel warning vocabularies inside one engine fragments naming and makes it harder for future tools to produce uniform diagnostic UX. (3) The discarded warning is an information-loss design smell: the function returns it but the only caller throws it away.
recommendation: Either unify to a shared `EngineWarning` (origin: 'registry' | 'output-style', kind, message, ...) and concatenate styleResult.warning into start.warnings, or rename to make the omission deliberate (e.g. document that outputStyleOk is the entire user-facing surface and remove the warning return from probeOutputStyle). The current state — produce-and-discard — is the worst of both worlds.

title: Registry duplicates PathInfo shape rather than re-using the schema's PathData
description: registry.ts declares `PathInfo` with the exact same five fields as `PathData` in schemas/path.ts (slug, title, summary, personalization_options, build_command), then manually copies field-by-field in the closing `paths.push({ ... })`. This is shape duplication: any future field added to path.json must be added in three places (PathData, PathInfo, and the manual copy), and a typo in the copy block silently drops a field. The registry is the consumer of the schema's parsed value — that value should be the source of truth.
impact: Naming/abstraction-layer leak: the schema layer's job is to parse-and-validate into a typed value; the registry layer's job is to aggregate and warn. Today registry.ts re-encodes the row shape, which couples it to path.json's field set. Adding a new path.json field in cycle 4 will require touching registry.ts even though no registry behavior changes.
recommendation: Either `export type PathInfo = PathData` (or just re-export PathData as PathInfo) and `paths.push(validation.value)` directly, or drop PathInfo and have scanRegistry return `{ paths: PathData[]; warnings: ... }`. The personalization_options narrowing from `PersonalizationOption[]` to `string[]` in PathInfo is also lossy — re-using PathData preserves the literal-union typing for downstream consumers.

title: scripts/e2e/harness.ts hard-codes the 'start' tool name and bypasses the MCP transport entirely
description: The harness's callTool dispatcher contains a literal `if (toolName === 'start')` branch that imports and calls runStart directly, then synthesizes an MCP-shaped { content: [{ type: 'text', text: JSON.stringify(...) }] } envelope. This means: (1) every future tool added in cycles 2–5 (preflight probe, lesson flow, etc.) requires editing harness.ts to add another if-branch — the harness has hard-coded knowledge of the tool registry that index.ts is supposed to own; (2) the harness never exercises the actual stdio transport, MCP framing, zod arg validation, or the server.tool registration in index.ts, so 'integration test' is misnamed — it's a thin wrapper around a direct function call. The contract (A12) says 'boots the MCP server'; this implementation does not boot the server.
impact: Two coupled hazards. (a) Cycle 2 will add runPreflightProbe and the harness will need a parallel branch — drift between index.ts's registered tools and harness.ts's mock-dispatcher is silent until a scenario fires. (b) Real MCP transport bugs (zod schema mismatch, argument shape, JSON-RPC envelope) cannot be caught by future scenarios because the harness never speaks MCP. The integration claim in T-028 is weaker than the test name suggests.
recommendation: Either (a) genuinely boot the MCP server: spawn `node dist/index.js` (or tsx) over stdio, connect a `Client` from `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/client/...`, and route callTool through it — then the harness has zero per-tool knowledge; or (b) re-use the same registration mechanism by having index.ts export a `registerTools(server)` factory that the harness can mount on an in-memory transport. Either way, remove the per-tool if-branch and the synthetic envelope.

title: index.ts inlines tool registration; no registerTools() seam for harness or future cycles
description: index.ts constructs the McpServer and registers the start tool inline at module top-level, then connects the stdio transport at top-level await. There is no exported function to register tools onto a caller-supplied server. Cycle 2 will add runPreflightProbe; that will mean appending another `server.tool(...)` block here, and the harness still cannot share the registration code (see R2-005). A small refactor — `export function registerTools(server: McpServer) { ... }` invoked from a `main()` that owns the stdio bootstrap — would isolate composition from transport and let the harness mount the same registrations on an in-memory transport.
impact: Mild but real: every new tool requires editing the same monolithic file, and the harness/in-process scenarios must duplicate registration logic. It also makes index.ts a side-effect module (import-for-effect), which complicates testing of the server bootstrap itself.
recommendation: Split: `tools/registerAll.ts` (or keep inline in index but as an exported function) takes the McpServer and wires up tools; `index.ts` becomes the bin script that constructs the server, calls registerAll, and connects stdio. The harness can then import registerAll and a different transport.

title: Registry uses synchronous fs APIs despite returning Promise<RegistryResult>
description: scanRegistry is declared `async` and returns `Promise<RegistryResult>`, but every fs operation inside is synchronous: readdirSync, existsSync, readFileSync. The async signature is purely cosmetic and misleads callers (e.g. start.ts awaits it as if I/O is overlapped with probeOutputStyle, but it isn't). Either the design wants async I/O (use fs.promises) or the function should be sync and the signature should reflect that. The current state is the awkward middle: API surface promises async semantics, implementation delivers blocking I/O.
impact: Future cycles that assume the registry is non-blocking (e.g. running it concurrently with another probe in cycle 2) will be silently surprised — the event loop stalls during the directory walk. It's also a naming/contract leak: an async function that does no async work is a low-grade design smell that compounds when the surface grows.
recommendation: Pick one. If the design wants async I/O (recommended for an MCP server), switch to `fs.promises.readdir`, `fs.promises.readFile`, `fs.promises.stat` — the existsSync check folds into a try/catch on stat or readFile. If sync is fine for cycle 1's tiny scan, drop `async` from the signature and return RegistryResult directly; let start.ts handle the lifting if it ever wants to parallelize.

title: start.ts owns the 'paths' subdirectory naming convention — leaks content layout into tool layer
description: runStart constructs `path.join(projectRoot, 'paths')` and passes it to scanRegistry. The 'paths' directory name — the convention that content lives under <projectRoot>/paths/<slug>/ — is a registry-layer concern, not a start-tool concern. If a future cycle wants to support a workspace with a configurable content root (e.g. an env override, or a multi-root repo), the change would have to be made in start.ts, not in registry.ts where the rest of the layout knowledge lives. The schema/registry boundary is otherwise clean; this one line breaks it.
impact: Mild. It's one line, but it's the kind of accidental coupling that makes the engine harder to reason about: 'where does the engine decide where content lives?' has two answers (start.ts joins 'paths', registry.ts walks it). If cycle 2 adds runPreflightProbe and that probe also needs to know the content root, the join will be duplicated.
recommendation: Move the 'paths' join into registry.ts: scanRegistry takes `projectRoot` and computes its own `paths/` subdirectory, OR export a small helper `resolvePathsRoot(projectRoot): string` from registry.ts and have callers use it. Either way, the convention lives in one place.
evidence:
const pathsRoot = path.join(projectRoot, 'paths');
  const registry = await scanRegistry(pathsRoot);

title: description.md in path content is unreferenced by engine — content/engine boundary slightly underspecified
description: The contract lists description.md as a per-path file ('short student-facing intro ≤ 200 words'), and the file exists, but no engine code reads it. The registry only validates path.json; PathInfo carries `summary` (short blurb) but not description body. This is fine for cycle 1 — description.md is presumably consumed by a future cycle's lesson-render path — but right now description.md is content with no contract: nothing validates that it exists, nothing surfaces a warning if it's missing, and a path with a deleted description.md still reports as healthy. Worth flagging so the next cycle either folds it into the registry warning surface or explicitly designates it as best-effort prose.
impact: No impact today. A potential design drift if cycle 4 quietly starts reading description.md without a corresponding registry-level contract — at that point a missing file becomes a runtime surprise rather than a structured warning.
recommendation: Either (a) add a 'missing-description-md' warning to the registry now (cheap, future-proofs the surface) or (b) add a one-liner comment in registry.ts noting that description.md is intentionally not validated at the engine layer. Defer the choice to whichever cycle first reads the file.
evidence:
# Orderbook Viewer

In this path you will build a real-time orderbook viewer for DeepBook v3

title: Magic string 'cycle-1' as the preflight reason is duplicated across contract, tool, and tests
description: The literal string 'cycle-1' appears as the preflight.reason. The StartResult interface narrows it to the literal type 'cycle-1', which is the right move. But this sentinel is intentionally going to be replaced in cycle 2, and cycle 2's reviewer will need to find every place this changes. A single exported constant (e.g. `export const PREFLIGHT_SKIPPED_CYCLE_1 = { skipped: true, reason: 'cycle-1' as const }`) would make the sentinel discoverable by name and centralize the cycle-bump.
impact: None today. A trivial maintenance smoothing for cycle 2.
recommendation: Optional: extract the preflight sentinel to a named constant. Skip if you'd rather keep the inline literal — this is genuinely info-level.
